description:
Road rules are laws that all road users in Tasmania must follow.

Drivers must obey traffic signs, road markings, traffic lights, and lawful directions.

Police officers and authorised traffic controllers have legal authority to direct traffic.

If a lawful direction conflicts with a sign or signal, the lawful direction must be followed.

Failing to obey road laws or lawful directions is an offence.

Drivers must exercise due care and attention while driving.

A driver must not operate a vehicle in a careless, dangerous, or reckless manner.

Drivers are responsible for helping prevent crashes and protecting other road users.

Safe driving requires concentration, observation, and responsible behaviour.

Unsafe driving may result in penalties or serious consequences.

Giving way means slowing down or stopping when necessary to avoid a collision.

Drivers must give way according to road rules, signs, and traffic conditions.

A driver entering a road from a driveway or parking position must give way to all traffic.

Drivers changing lanes must give way to vehicles already in the lane.

Failing to give way when required is an offence.

An uncontrolled intersection has no traffic lights or regulatory signs.

At uncontrolled intersections, drivers must give way to vehicles approaching from the right.

At a T-intersection, drivers on the terminating road must give way to traffic on the continuing road unless signs indicate otherwise.

Drivers should approach intersections cautiously and be prepared to stop.

Careful observation improves safety at intersections.

Drivers must use indicators to communicate their intentions to other road users.

Indicators are required when turning, changing lanes, merging, or moving from a parked position.

Signals should be given early enough to provide warning to others.

Correct signalling helps reduce confusion and crashes.

Failing to indicate properly may result in penalties.

Drivers must not enter an intersection unless there is enough room to leave it completely.

Blocking intersections can disrupt traffic and obstruct emergency vehicles.

Even with a green light, drivers should wait if traffic ahead prevents safe exit.

Drivers should assess traffic conditions before entering intersections.

Responsible driving helps maintain traffic flow and safety.

Temporary road rules apply in roadwork and traffic management areas.

Drivers must obey temporary speed limits, signs, barriers, and directions from traffic controllers.

Roadwork zones may contain workers, machinery, changed lanes, or uneven surfaces.

Extra caution is required when travelling through roadwork areas.

Ignoring temporary traffic controls may result in penalties.

Drivers are legally responsible for following all road rules and licence conditions.

A driver must ensure their vehicle is safe and roadworthy before driving.

Breaking road laws may result in fines, demerit points, licence suspension, or court action.

Safe and lawful driving helps protect all road users.

Responsible behaviour contributes to safer roads across Tasmania.

Regulatory signs inform drivers of road rules and legal requirements.

Examples include STOP, GIVE WAY, NO ENTRY, and turn restriction signs.

Drivers must obey regulatory signs unless directed otherwise by police or authorised traffic controllers.

Ignoring a regulatory sign may result in penalties.

Regulatory signs help maintain safe and orderly traffic flow.

Speed signs display the maximum legal speed allowed on a road.

Drivers must not exceed the posted speed limit.

Temporary or variable speed signs may apply in certain conditions or locations.

Drivers should reduce speed further when conditions are unsafe.

Safe speed choice improves vehicle control and road safety.

Warning signs alert drivers to hazards or changing road conditions ahead.

Examples include curves, intersections, wildlife crossings, and slippery roads.

Drivers should slow down and prepare to respond safely when approaching warning signs.

Ignoring warning signs can increase crash risk.

Warning signs improve driver awareness and safety.

Traffic lights control the movement of vehicles and pedestrians.

Drivers must stop at a red light and proceed only when permitted.

A yellow light means drivers should stop safely if possible before the intersection.

Drivers must not enter an intersection if their path is blocked.

Traffic lights improve traffic flow and safety at intersections.

Pedestrian signals indicate when pedestrians may safely cross the road.

Drivers must give way to pedestrians crossing lawfully.

Extra care is required near pedestrian crossings and intersections.

Drivers should remain alert for pedestrians entering crossings unexpectedly.

Pedestrian safety is an important part of road safety.

Temporary signs provide instructions and warnings in roadwork or changed traffic conditions.

Drivers must obey temporary speed limits, lane closures, and directions from traffic controllers.

Roadwork areas may contain workers, machinery, and altered road surfaces.

Extra caution is required when travelling through temporary traffic arrangements.

Ignoring temporary signs may result in penalties.

Lane control signals indicate whether lanes are open, closed, or restricted.

A green arrow means the lane may be used.

A red X means drivers must not drive in that lane.

Drivers must obey lane control signals and move safely when required.

Lane signals help improve traffic safety and efficiency.

Railway signals warn drivers of approaching trains and level crossings.

Drivers must stop when warning lights flash, bells sound, or boom gates are down or lowering.

A railway crossing should not be entered unless there is enough room to clear it safely.

Ignoring railway signals is dangerous and unlawful.

Drivers should approach railway crossings with caution.

Advisory and guide signs provide information about routes, destinations, and road conditions.

Guide signs help drivers navigate safely and plan ahead.

Advisory speed signs recommend safer speeds for specific road conditions.

Drivers should consider advisory information to improve safety.

These signs support safe and efficient travel.

Drivers must obey all traffic signs, signals, and road markings.

Traffic signals are designed to manage traffic safely and efficiently.

If traffic lights are not operating, drivers must follow normal giving way rules.

Police officers or authorised traffic controllers may override traffic signals.

Careful observation and compliance improve road safety.

Speed limits are the maximum legal speeds permitted on roads.

Drivers must obey posted speed limits at all times.

Driving above the speed limit increases crash risk and stopping distance.

Drivers must also reduce speed when conditions make the posted limit unsafe.

Safe speed choice improves control and road safety.

School zones operate during the times shown on school zone signs.

Drivers must obey reduced speed limits in active school zones.

Extra care is required because children may behave unpredictably near roads.

Drivers should be prepared to stop safely when children are present.

School zone rules help protect vulnerable road users.

Shared zones are areas where vehicles and pedestrians share the same road space.

Drivers must travel at or below the posted shared zone speed limit.

Pedestrians generally have priority in shared zones.

Drivers should be prepared to stop safely at any time.

Careful driving improves safety in shared areas.

Temporary speed limits apply in roadwork and construction zones.

Drivers must obey all temporary signs and directions from traffic controllers.

Roadwork areas may contain workers, machinery, lane changes, or uneven surfaces.

Extra caution is required when travelling through work zones.

Ignoring work zone rules may result in penalties.

Drivers must adjust speed to suit weather and road conditions.

Rain, fog, smoke, and strong winds can reduce visibility and vehicle control.

Driving too fast for conditions increases crash risk.

Drivers should slow down and increase following distance in poor weather.

Safe speed choice improves vehicle control and safety.

Drivers must leave enough distance behind the vehicle ahead to stop safely.

Safe following distance provides more time to react to hazards.

Following too closely, known as tailgating, increases crash risk.

Larger gaps are needed at higher speeds and in poor conditions.

Maintaining safe space improves road safety.

Stopping distance is the total distance travelled before a vehicle comes to a complete stop.

Stopping distance includes reaction distance and braking distance.

Higher speeds increase the distance needed to stop safely.

Wet or loose road surfaces can greatly increase stopping distances.

Drivers should always allow enough stopping space.

Drivers should choose a speed suitable for traffic, road, and weather conditions.

The posted speed limit is the maximum legal speed, not always the safest speed.

Driving at a safe speed improves control and reduces crash severity.

Drivers must be able to stop safely within the visible distance ahead.

Responsible speed choice helps protect all road users.

Drivers must obey road rules and operate vehicles safely at all times.

A driver is responsible for controlling the vehicle and avoiding danger to others.

Drivers must follow traffic signs, signals, road markings, and lawful directions.

Unsafe or unlawful driving can place other road users at risk.

Responsible driving improves road safety for everyone.

Drivers involved in a crash must stop and provide assistance where required.

Drivers may need to exchange details with other people involved.

Police should be notified in situations involving injury, danger, or major damage.

Leaving the scene of a crash without meeting legal responsibilities is an offence.

Drivers should remain calm and act safely after a crash.

Drivers and passengers must wear seatbelts where fitted.

Children must be secured in approved restraints suitable for their age and size.

Drivers are responsible for ensuring restraint rules are followed where required.

Seatbelts and child restraints reduce injury risk during crashes.

Correct restraint use improves safety for all occupants.

Using mobile phones or devices while driving can distract attention from the road.

Drivers must follow the mobile phone rules that apply to their licence type.

Learner and provisional drivers may face stricter restrictions.

Avoiding distractions improves concentration and road safety.

Drivers should keep full attention on driving at all times.

Drivers must ensure their vehicle is safe and roadworthy before driving.

Important safety features include tyres, brakes, lights, mirrors, and indicators.

Unsafe vehicles can increase crash risk and may be unlawful.

Regular inspections and maintenance improve vehicle safety.

Drivers are responsible for the condition of their vehicle.

Fatigue reduces concentration, reaction time, and decision-making ability.

Drivers should avoid driving when tired or sleepy.

Long trips, night driving, and lack of sleep increase fatigue risk.

Taking regular breaks helps reduce tiredness.

If a driver feels drowsy, the safest option is to stop and rest.

Learner and provisional drivers must follow all licence conditions.

Conditions may include displaying plates, obeying speed restrictions, and following mobile phone rules.

Licence conditions are legal requirements and must be obeyed at all times.

Breaking licence conditions may result in penalties or licence action.

Safe behaviour helps new drivers build experience responsibly.

Supervising drivers must meet the legal requirements for supervising learner drivers.

A supervisor should help the learner develop safe driving habits and skills.

Supervising drivers must not be affected by alcohol, drugs, or fatigue.

Clear communication and calm guidance improve learner safety.

Responsible supervision supports safer roads for everyone.

Blood Alcohol Concentration, or BAC, measures the amount of alcohol in a person's bloodstream.

Alcohol affects judgement, coordination, concentration, and reaction time.

Learner and provisional drivers are generally required to maintain zero BAC while driving.

Driving with an illegal BAC increases crash risk and may result in severe penalties.

Safe drivers avoid alcohol before driving.

Police may conduct random breath testing to detect drink driving.

Drivers must stop and provide a breath sample when lawfully directed.

Refusing to participate in a breath test is an offence.

Random breath testing helps reduce alcohol-related crashes.

Cooperation with lawful testing improves road safety.

Illegal drugs and some medicines can impair driving ability.

Certain medicines may cause drowsiness, slower reactions, or reduced concentration.

Drivers must not drive while affected by drugs or medication.

Medicine labels and medical advice should be checked before driving.

Driving while impaired increases crash risk and may be unlawful.

Fatigue can seriously reduce alertness, reaction time, and decision making.

Lack of sleep, long trips, and night driving increase fatigue risk.

Drivers should take regular breaks and avoid driving when sleepy.

Falling asleep at the wheel can cause serious crashes.

Resting before driving improves safety.

Some medical conditions may affect a person's ability to drive safely.

Conditions affecting vision, movement, awareness, or concentration can impact driving.

Drivers may need to report certain medical conditions to the licensing authority.

Medical advice should be followed to help ensure safe driving.

Responsible management of health conditions improves road safety.

Drivers must be physically and mentally fit to drive safely.

Illness, fatigue, alcohol, drugs, stress, or distraction can reduce driving ability.

A driver should not drive if they cannot concentrate fully or control the vehicle safely.

Being fit to drive helps protect drivers, passengers, and other road users.

Responsible decisions improve road safety.

Hazard perception involves identifying and responding to potential dangers early.

Drivers should continuously scan the road ahead, mirrors, and surrounding traffic.

Good hazard perception provides more time to react safely.

Anticipating hazards helps reduce crash risk.

Careful observation improves road safety.

A space cushion is the safe distance maintained around a vehicle.

Leaving space in front, behind, and beside the vehicle improves safety.

A larger space cushion provides more time to react to hazards.

Drivers should increase space in poor weather or heavy traffic.

Maintaining safe space helps reduce crash risk.

Drivers should choose a speed suitable for road, traffic, and weather conditions.

The posted speed limit is the maximum legal speed, not always the safest speed.

Safe speed choice improves vehicle control and reduces stopping distance.

Drivers should slow down when visibility or road conditions are poor.

Responsible speed choice improves safety for all road users.

Wet roads reduce tyre grip and increase stopping distances.

Drivers should slow down and leave extra following distance in rainy conditions.

Sudden braking or steering can cause skidding or loss of control.

Headlights should be used when visibility is reduced.

Careful driving improves safety in wet weather.

Night driving reduces visibility and makes hazards harder to detect.

Drivers should use headlights correctly and remain alert for pedestrians, cyclists, and animals.

Fatigue risk is often higher at night.

Drivers should slow down and maintain safe following distances.

Careful observation improves safety during night driving.

Drivers should maintain a safe following distance behind other vehicles.

Smooth and controlled braking helps maintain vehicle stability.

Following too closely increases the risk of rear-end crashes.

Extra braking distance is needed at higher speeds and in poor conditions.

Safe following and braking improve road safety.

Defensive driving means anticipating hazards and preparing for the actions of other road users.

Drivers should remain alert, patient, and aware of changing traffic conditions.

Safe defensive driving includes leaving space and avoiding unnecessary risks.

Defensive driving helps reduce crash risk.

Responsible driving improves safety for everyone.

Intersections are high-risk areas that require careful observation.

Drivers should approach intersections at safe speeds and be prepared to give way.

Checking for vehicles, pedestrians, and cyclists is essential before proceeding.

Many crashes occur at intersections because of poor observation.

Careful driving improves intersection safety.

Animals, loose gravel, potholes, and slippery surfaces can create driving hazards.

Drivers should slow down and remain alert in hazardous areas.

Sudden steering or braking may cause loss of vehicle control.

Road and wildlife warning signs should be taken seriously.

Adjusting driving to conditions improves safety.

Drivers should adjust seats and mirrors before driving.

Correct seating position improves comfort, visibility, and vehicle control.

Mirrors should provide the clearest possible view around the vehicle.

Proper adjustment helps reduce blind spots.

Good driving position improves awareness and safety.

Drivers must give way according to road rules, signs, traffic lights, and road conditions.

At intersections, drivers should slow down and check carefully for other vehicles, pedestrians, and cyclists.

Failing to give way can lead to serious crashes.

Drivers must be prepared to stop if necessary to avoid a collision.

Careful observation improves intersection safety.

Drivers must turn from and into the correct lane where required.

Indicators must be used before turning.

Drivers should check mirrors and blind spots before turning.

Pedestrians and cyclists must be considered when completing turns.

Safe turning requires good observation and correct positioning.

Slip lanes allow vehicles to turn without entering the main intersection.

Drivers using slip lanes must give way to pedestrians crossing the lane.

Drivers should approach slip lanes at safe speeds.

Cyclists and pedestrians may be difficult to see near slip lanes.

Careful observation improves safety in slip lane areas.

Drivers approaching a roundabout must give way to vehicles already in the roundabout.

Drivers should choose the correct lane before entering.

Indicators should be used correctly when exiting where required.

Drivers must travel at safe speeds and remain alert.

Roundabouts improve traffic flow and safety when used correctly.

Drivers must not enter an intersection unless there is enough room to clear it safely.

Blocking intersections can disrupt traffic and delay emergency vehicles.

Even with a green light, drivers should wait if traffic ahead prevents safe exit.

Drivers should assess traffic conditions before entering intersections.

Responsible driving improves traffic flow and safety.

Drivers must give way to pedestrians where required by road rules.

Extra care is needed near crossings, school areas, and busy intersections.

Pedestrians may enter crossings unexpectedly.

Drivers should slow down and remain alert around pedestrian areas.

Protecting pedestrians is an important part of road safety.

Drivers turning right must give way to oncoming traffic and pedestrians where required.

Right turns should be made from the correct lane and with proper signalling.

Drivers should only begin turning when it is safe to do so.

Careful observation is essential when turning across traffic.

Safe turning reduces crash risk.

Drivers turning left must signal and position the vehicle correctly before turning.

Drivers should check for pedestrians, cyclists, and vehicles before completing the turn.

Left turns should be made carefully and at safe speeds.

Observation and lane positioning are important for safe left turns.

Careful driving improves safety for all road users.

Roundabouts can present hazards such as incorrect lane use, failure to give way, and sudden lane changes.

Drivers should remain alert for cyclists, pedestrians, and large vehicles.

Entering too quickly can reduce vehicle control.

Safe roundabout driving requires patience and careful observation.

Responsible driving improves safety at roundabouts.

Drivers should approach all intersections with caution.

Checking traffic from all directions helps identify hazards early.

Intersections are common locations for crashes because multiple road users interact.

Safe speeds, observation, and correct giving way are essential.

Careful driving improves intersection safety for everyone.

Lane markings help organise traffic and guide drivers safely on the road.

Drivers must obey continuous lines, broken lines, arrows, and painted road markings.

Crossing certain lane markings may be prohibited except in specific situations.

Road markings improve traffic flow and reduce crash risk.

Drivers should remain alert to changing lane markings.

Drivers should use the correct lane for their direction and intended movement.

Some lanes may be reserved for turning, buses, bicycles, or other special uses.

Drivers must obey lane arrows and lane control signs.

Correct lane use improves traffic flow and safety.

Planning ahead helps drivers select the appropriate lane safely.

Drivers must only overtake when it is safe and legal to do so.

Before overtaking, drivers should check mirrors, blind spots, and oncoming traffic.

Overtaking may be prohibited near intersections, crossings, or continuous lines.

Drivers must return safely to their lane after overtaking.

Unsafe overtaking increases crash risk.

Merging occurs when two lanes combine into one.

Drivers should merge smoothly and safely without forcing other vehicles to brake suddenly.

When lane lines end, drivers must give way according to road rules.

Signalling and observation help safe merging.

Careful merging improves traffic flow and safety.

Special lanes may include bus lanes, bicycle lanes, transit lanes, and turning lanes.

Drivers must obey signs and markings relating to special lanes.

Only permitted vehicles may use certain special lanes.

Improper use of special lanes may result in penalties.

Special lanes help improve traffic efficiency and safety.

Drivers may be required to keep left on multi-lane roads unless overtaking.

Keeping left helps traffic move safely and efficiently.

Drivers should avoid unnecessarily remaining in the right lane.

Road signs may indicate where keep left rules apply.

Following keep left rules improves traffic flow.

Drivers changing lanes must give way to vehicles already in the lane.

Before changing lanes, drivers should check mirrors, blind spots, and signal appropriately.

Lane changes should be made smoothly and only when safe.

Unsafe lane changes can cause crashes and disrupt traffic.

Good observation improves lane change safety.

Safe overtaking requires clear visibility, adequate space, and good judgement.

Drivers should avoid overtaking near intersections, curves, crests, or pedestrian crossings.

Overtaking should be completed efficiently and safely.

Drivers must ensure they can return safely to their lane.

Patience and observation improve overtaking safety.

Drivers should remain aware of surrounding traffic and maintain safe lane positioning.

Correct lane use reduces confusion and improves road safety.

Drivers should avoid sudden movements that may surprise other road users.

Observation and planning are important for safe lane driving.

Careful driving helps reduce crash risk.

Safe driving requires observation, planning, patience, and responsible behaviour.

Drivers should anticipate hazards and adjust driving to suit conditions.

Maintaining safe distances and obeying road rules helps reduce crash risk.

Responsible driving protects all road users.

Careful decisions improve safety on all roads.

Stopping and parking have different meanings under road rules.

A vehicle is considered parked when left stationary, even briefly, unless actively picking up or dropping off passengers.

Drivers must obey all signs and restrictions relating to stopping and parking.

Illegal stopping or parking can obstruct traffic and create hazards.

Understanding the difference helps drivers comply with road rules.

Drivers must not stop in a no stopping zone unless required by traffic conditions or emergencies.

No stopping signs help keep traffic moving safely and efficiently.

Stopping briefly to pick up or drop off passengers is not allowed in these zones.

Ignoring no stopping restrictions may result in penalties.

Drivers should always check roadside signs before stopping.

Drivers may stop briefly in a no parking zone only where permitted for passenger pick-up or drop-off.

The driver must remain near the vehicle and move it promptly when required.

Leaving the vehicle unattended is generally not permitted.

No parking restrictions help maintain traffic flow and safety.

Drivers should obey all parking signs and road markings.

Stopping or parking is not allowed in a clearway during the times shown on signs.

Clearways are designed to improve traffic flow during busy periods.

Vehicles parked illegally in clearways may be fined or towed away.

Drivers should check clearway signs carefully before parking.

Obeying clearway rules helps reduce congestion.

Drivers must not stop or park in bus zones unless permitted by signs.

Bus stops and bus zones allow buses to pick up and drop off passengers safely.

Stopping in these areas can obstruct public transport and create hazards.

Drivers should remain alert for buses entering or leaving bus zones.

Careful driving improves safety around buses.

Drivers must not stop or park across driveways or too close to intersections.

Parking restrictions near intersections improve visibility and safety.

Blocking driveways may prevent access for residents and emergency services.

Drivers should always check signs and road markings before parking.

Safe parking helps reduce hazards for all road users.

Loading zones are reserved for vehicles permitted by the displayed signs.

These zones are intended for loading or unloading goods or passengers where allowed.

Drivers must comply with all conditions applying to loading zones.

Improper use of loading zones may result in penalties.

Loading zones help support safe and efficient traffic movement.

Vehicles must be parked safely and within marked parking spaces where provided.

Drivers should park close to the kerb and avoid obstructing traffic.

Parking on footpaths, nature strips, or across lines may be illegal.

Correct parking position improves safety and visibility.

Drivers should secure the vehicle before leaving it unattended.

Drivers may stop in restricted areas if necessary because of an emergency.

Emergency stopping should only occur when absolutely necessary for safety.

Hazard lights may be used to warn other road users.

Drivers should move the vehicle to a safer location as soon as possible.

Remaining aware of surrounding traffic improves safety during emergencies.

Drivers should park where the vehicle does not create danger or obstruct traffic.

Checking signs, road markings, and local restrictions helps avoid penalties.

Vehicles should be secured properly before the driver leaves.

Safe parking helps protect pedestrians, cyclists, and other road users.

Responsible parking improves overall road safety.

Drivers must give way to pedestrians in situations required by road rules.

Pedestrians may be crossing at intersections, crossings, shared zones, or slip lanes.

Drivers should slow down and remain alert near pedestrian areas.

Pedestrians are vulnerable because they have little protection in crashes.

Protecting pedestrians is an important part of road safety.

Children can behave unpredictably near roads and crossings.

Drivers must take extra care near schools, playgrounds, and school crossings.

School zones operate at reduced speed limits during signed times.

Drivers should be prepared to stop quickly when children are nearby.

Careful driving helps protect young road users.

Cyclists are legitimate road users and must be treated with care and respect.

Drivers should check carefully for cyclists before turning, changing lanes, or opening vehicle doors.

Cyclists may move within a lane to avoid hazards.

Drivers should allow cyclists enough space to travel safely.

Awareness of cyclists improves road safety.

Bicycle lanes are reserved primarily for cyclists.

Drivers must obey signs and markings relating to bicycle lanes.

Before crossing or entering a bicycle lane, drivers should check carefully for cyclists.

Extra caution is needed near intersections and turning points.

Careful observation helps reduce crashes involving cyclists.

Shared paths are used by pedestrians, cyclists, and other approved users.

Drivers crossing shared paths should watch carefully for approaching pedestrians and cyclists.

Different users may travel at different speeds on shared paths.

Awareness and caution help reduce conflicts and collisions.

Safe interaction improves safety for everyone.

Elderly and disabled pedestrians may require more time to cross roads safely.

Drivers should remain patient and prepared to stop when necessary.

Mobility aids and assistance devices may affect pedestrian movement.

Extra care helps protect vulnerable road users.

Responsible driving improves safety for all pedestrians.

Drivers must leave sufficient space when overtaking cyclists.

Safe passing distance reduces the risk of collisions and intimidation.

Drivers should only overtake cyclists when it is safe and legal to do so.

Patience and careful observation improve overtaking safety.

Giving cyclists space improves road safety for everyone.

Drivers should remain alert for animals and other vulnerable road users near roads.

Wildlife, pets, and livestock may enter the road unexpectedly.

Drivers should slow down in areas where animal warning signs are displayed.

Safe driving helps reduce injury to people and animals.

Careful observation improves safety in rural and urban areas.

Drivers should always consider the safety of vulnerable road users.

Pedestrians, cyclists, children, and motorcyclists are less protected in crashes.

Careful observation, safe speeds, and patience help reduce crash risk.

Responsible driving improves safety for everyone on the road.

Safe behaviour helps create safer communities.

Good driver awareness involves constantly checking for hazards and vulnerable road users.

Drivers should use mirrors, scan intersections, and check blind spots regularly.

Distraction or poor observation increases crash risk.

Remaining alert helps drivers respond safely to changing conditions.

Awareness and concentration improve road safety.

Roadwork areas may contain workers, machinery, lane closures, and changed traffic conditions.

Drivers must obey temporary signs, speed limits, and directions from authorised traffic controllers.

Road surfaces in work zones may be uneven or hazardous.

Extra caution is required when travelling through roadwork areas.

Careful driving helps protect workers and other road users.

Drivers must obey all signals, lights, bells, and boom gates at railway level crossings.

Drivers must stop when warning signals operate or when a train is approaching.

A crossing should not be entered unless there is enough room to clear it safely.

Ignoring railway crossing rules is dangerous and unlawful.

Drivers should approach crossings carefully and remain alert.

Drivers should switch on headlights when travelling through tunnels.

Stopping in a tunnel should only occur in emergencies.

Drivers must obey tunnel signs, lane signals, and speed limits.

Maintaining safe following distance is important in tunnel environments.

Careful driving improves safety in confined traffic areas.

Drivers entering motorways should merge safely with traffic already travelling on the motorway.

Acceleration lanes help drivers match motorway traffic speed.

Drivers should plan lane changes early and maintain safe following distances.

Motorway ramps may require reduced speed because of curves or traffic conditions.

Safe merging and lane discipline improve motorway safety.

Rain, fog, wind, storms, and dust can reduce visibility and vehicle control.

Drivers should slow down and increase following distance in poor weather.

Headlights may be needed when visibility is reduced.

Driving too fast for conditions increases crash risk.

Careful driving improves safety in adverse weather.

Sun glare can reduce visibility and make hazards difficult to see.

Drivers should slow down and use sun visors or suitable eyewear when needed.

Extra care is required during sunrise and sunset.

Reduced visibility increases stopping distance and crash risk.

Drivers should remain alert in bright conditions.

Animals may enter roads unexpectedly, especially in rural or remote areas.

Drivers should slow down in areas marked with animal warning signs.

Sudden swerving may cause loss of vehicle control.

Careful observation and reduced speed improve safety around animals.

Drivers should remain alert during dawn and dusk when wildlife activity may increase.

If a vehicle breaks down, drivers should move to a safe location if possible.

Hazard lights may be used to warn other road users.

Drivers and passengers should remain aware of surrounding traffic.

Emergency situations require calm and careful decision making.

Safe actions help reduce further danger on the roadside.

Fog, smoke, heavy rain, dust, and darkness can reduce visibility.

Drivers should slow down and increase following distance in low visibility conditions.

Headlights should be used appropriately to improve visibility.

Drivers must be able to stop safely within the visible distance ahead.

Careful driving helps reduce crash risk in poor visibility.

Drivers may encounter unusual or changing road conditions that require extra caution.

Safe driving involves adjusting speed, observation, and behaviour to suit conditions.

Planning ahead and remaining alert help reduce risks.

Careful driving improves safety in all road environments.

Responsible behaviour helps protect all road users.

Drivers must give way to emergency vehicles displaying flashing lights or sounding sirens.

Emergency vehicles may include police, ambulance, and fire service vehicles.

Drivers should move safely to allow emergency vehicles to pass.

Sudden or unsafe movements should be avoided.

Cooperating safely helps emergency services respond quickly.

Emergency vehicles may approach intersections against normal traffic flow during emergencies.

Drivers should check carefully and give way when safe to do so.

Drivers must not block intersections when emergency vehicles are approaching.

Careful observation is important near intersections involving emergency vehicles.

Safe cooperation improves emergency response safety.

Police officers have legal authority to direct traffic.

Drivers must obey lawful directions given by police officers.

Police directions may override traffic lights, signs, or road markings.

Failing to obey police directions is an offence.

Compliance helps maintain safety and traffic control.

Drivers must stop when directed by police.

Police may require drivers to stop for enforcement, safety checks, or emergencies.

Drivers should stop safely and follow lawful instructions.

Failing to stop for police may result in serious penalties.

Responsible cooperation improves safety for everyone.

Police may conduct roadside breath and drug testing.

Drivers must comply with lawful testing requirements.

Refusing a test or failing to follow instructions is an offence.

Testing helps improve road safety and reduce impaired driving.

Cooperation with testing supports safer roads.

Police and emergency personnel may direct traffic around crashes or incidents.

Drivers must obey lawful directions at accident scenes.

Temporary traffic arrangements may apply during emergencies.

Careful driving helps protect emergency workers and other road users.

Drivers should remain alert near incident areas.

Emergency vehicles may travel quickly or unexpectedly through traffic when responding to emergencies.

Drivers should remain alert for flashing lights and sirens.

Drivers should not follow closely behind emergency vehicles.

Safe cooperation helps emergency services reach incidents quickly.

Awareness improves safety for all road users.

Failing to obey emergency vehicle or police directions can result in fines, demerit points, or other penalties.

Road laws are designed to protect emergency workers and the public.

Unsafe behaviour around emergency situations increases crash risk.

Drivers are legally responsible for complying with lawful instructions.

Responsible behaviour improves road safety.

Drivers should remain calm and alert during emergencies or unusual traffic situations.

Awareness of emergency vehicles, incidents, and changing conditions improves safety.

Drivers should slow down and follow directions carefully near emergency scenes.

Safe behaviour helps protect emergency personnel and other road users.

Responsible driving improves safety during emergencies.

Seatbelts help protect drivers and passengers from serious injury during crashes.

Drivers and passengers must wear seatbelts where fitted.

Seatbelts should be worn correctly and adjusted properly.

Wearing a seatbelt improves safety during sudden braking or collisions.

Correct seatbelt use is an important road safety requirement.

Drivers are responsible for ensuring passengers comply with restraint rules where required.

Children must be secured in suitable approved restraints.

Drivers should check restraints before driving.

Failing to meet restraint responsibilities may result in penalties.

Responsible drivers help protect all vehicle occupants.

Children must use approved restraints suitable for their age and size.

Child restraints include rear-facing restraints, forward-facing restraints, and booster seats.

Correct installation and adjustment are important for safety.

Using appropriate restraints reduces injury risk during crashes.

Drivers should ensure restraints are used properly.

Restrictions may apply to children travelling in the front seat.

Airbags can increase risk to young children if restraints are unsuitable.

Drivers should ensure front seat passengers wear seatbelts correctly.

Passengers should remain seated properly while the vehicle is moving.

Safe seating arrangements improve occupant protection.

Passengers should behave safely and avoid distracting the driver.

Unsafe passenger behaviour can increase crash risk.

Passengers should keep body parts inside the vehicle while travelling.

Responsible passenger behaviour improves safety for everyone.

Drivers should encourage safe conduct inside the vehicle.

Limited exemptions to seatbelt or restraint rules may apply in special situations.

Drivers should always aim to minimise risk to passengers.

Seatbelt laws are designed to reduce injury and save lives.

Drivers are responsible for complying with restraint requirements.

Responsible behaviour improves road safety.

Seatbelts and restraints are essential safety devices.

Drivers should encourage safe behaviour by all passengers.

Road safety depends on everyone using restraints properly.

Seatbelt and restraint requirements apply across different vehicle types where fitted.

Drivers should regularly check that restraints and seatbelts are in good condition.

Drivers must ensure their vehicle is roadworthy before driving.

A roadworthy vehicle is safe to operate and meets legal safety standards.

Unsafe or defective vehicles increase crash risk.

Regular inspections and repairs help maintain vehicle safety.

Drivers are responsible for the condition of their vehicle.

Tyres must be in safe condition with adequate tread depth.

Drivers should inspect tyres regularly for wear or defects.

Safe tyres improve vehicle control and road safety.

Brakes are essential for controlling speed and stopping safely.

Drivers should ensure brakes are functioning correctly before driving.

Vehicle lights and indicators help drivers see and communicate with other road users.

Drivers should ensure headlights, brake lights, indicators, and tail lights work properly.

Drivers must maintain a clear view through the windscreen and mirrors.

Warning indicators alert drivers to possible vehicle problems.

Drivers should understand the meaning of dashboard warning lights.

Ignoring warning indicators may lead to unsafe driving or mechanical failure.

Vehicles should be checked promptly when warning lights appear.

Responding early helps maintain vehicle safety.

Vehicles require correct fluid levels for safe operation.

Important fluids include engine oil, coolant, brake fluid, and windscreen washer fluid.

Low or contaminated fluids can damage the vehicle or reduce safety.

Drivers should check fluid levels regularly.

Proper maintenance improves vehicle reliability and safety.

Loads must be secured safely and must not obstruct the driver's view.

Loose or poorly secured loads can create hazards for other road users.

Drivers should ensure mirrors, windows, and lights remain visible.

Safe loading improves vehicle stability and control.

Careful load management improves road safety.

Regular vehicle maintenance helps prevent breakdowns and unsafe conditions.

Drivers should follow recommended servicing schedules.

Routine inspections can identify problems before they become dangerous.

Good maintenance habits improve reliability and safety.

Responsible maintenance reduces crash risk.

Vehicle safety depends on proper maintenance, safe driving, and regular inspections.

Attention to vehicle condition reduces crash risk.

Safe vehicles help protect drivers, passengers, and other road users.

Loads must be secured safely to prevent movement or falling during travel.

Loose loads can create serious hazards for other road users.

Drivers should check restraints and load stability before driving.

Proper load security improves vehicle control and road safety.

Drivers are responsible for ensuring loads remain secure.

Vehicles and trailers must not exceed legal load and weight limits.

Safe towing reduces crash risk.

Trailers must have required safety equipment fitted and working correctly.

Drivers should inspect trailer equipment before driving.

Faulty trailer equipment can create serious safety hazards.

Regular checks improve towing safety.

Heavy vehicles require more space and longer distances to stop safely.

Drivers should avoid travelling in heavy vehicle blind spots.

Extra caution is needed when overtaking or merging near heavy vehicles.

Large vehicles may require additional room for turning.

Understanding heavy vehicle limitations improves road safety.

Vehicles carrying loads or towing trailers may handle differently.

Drivers should reduce speed and avoid sudden steering or braking.

Extra care is needed on curves, slopes, and in strong winds.

Safe driving behaviour helps maintain control of loaded vehicles.

Careful driving improves towing and load safety.

Drivers are legally responsible for ensuring loads and trailers are safe and compliant.

Drivers must obey all relevant towing and load regulations.

Different licence classes allow drivers to operate different types of vehicles.

Drivers must hold the correct licence class for the vehicle they are driving.

Licence classes may include cars, motorcycles, heavy vehicles, and other vehicle types.

Driving without the correct licence class is an offence.

Understanding licence classes helps ensure safe and lawful driving.

Learner drivers must comply with all learner licence conditions.

Learners are required to drive under supervision by an appropriately licensed driver.

Learner drivers must obey road rules and display L-plates correctly.

Safe supervised practice helps develop driving skills and experience.

Responsible behaviour improves learner driver safety.

Learner drivers must clearly display L-plates while driving.

L-plates help other road users identify learner drivers.

Plates should be visible from the front and rear of the vehicle as required.

Failing to display L-plates correctly may result in penalties.

Correct display supports safer driving conditions.

Provisional drivers must comply with the conditions applying to their licence.

Conditions may include restrictions relating to alcohol, mobile phones, passengers, or speed.

Breaking provisional licence conditions may result in penalties or licence action.

Responsible driving helps provisional drivers gain experience safely.

Using a mobile phone while driving can distract attention from the road.

Passenger restrictions may apply to some provisional drivers.

Drivers must understand and comply with any passenger conditions attached to their licence.
